A coupling module composed of (trimethylsilyl)ethynyl and chlorophenyl groups selectively accelerated sluggish desilylative on-surface Sonogashira coupling with homocoupling efficiently suppressed. Bond-resolved scanning tunneling microscopy/atomic force microscopy (STM/AFM) with a CO-functionalized tip enabled investigation of this coupling reaction and evaluation of its chemoselectivity (see picture).
On-surface synthesis is a powerful method for the fabrication of π-conjugated nanomaterials. Herein, we demonstrate chemoselective Sonogashira coupling between (trimethylsilyl)ethynyl and chlorophenyl groups in silylethynyl- and chloro-substituted partially fluorinated phenylene ethynylenes (SiCPFPEs) on Ag(111). The desilylative Sonogashira coupling occurred with high chemoselectivity up to 75 %, while the competing Ullmann and desilylative Glaser homocoupling reactions were suppressed. A combination of bond-resolved scanning tunneling microscopy/atomic force microscopy (STM/AFM) and DFT calculations revealed that the oligomers were obtained by the formation of intermolecular silylene tethers (-Me2Si-) through CH3−Si bond activation at 130 °C and subsequent elimination of the tethers at an elevated temperature of 200 °C.
On-surface synthesis has received considerable attention because atomically defined carbon nanomaterials can be fabricated. In 2007, Grill, Hecht, and co-workers reported the first example of on-surface Ullmann coupling between 4-bromo-substituted tetraphenylporphyrins.1 The carbon–carbon bond formation is usually promoted with a catalysis of surface metal atoms at an elevated temperature.2 To date several on-surface reactions have been developed, such as Ullmann,1, 3 Glaser,4 and dehydrogenative couplings,5 Bergman cyclization,6 dehydrative formation of a boronic ester,7 and so on. These reactions with predesigned precursors lead to formations of various π-conjugated nanomaterials such as covalent organic frameworks,1, 7b, 8 graphene nanoribbons (GNRs),9 and nanographenes.10 Furthermore, GNR heterojunctions have been also fabricated by the consecutive Ullmann coupling of two kinds of dibromoarenes.11 Although dehalogenative and/or oxidative homocoupling protocols are successfully employed in the construction of π-expanded motifs, examples of the on-surface Sonogashira coupling are still scarce in the literature.12
The Sonogashira coupling is a powerful methodology for syntheses of arylethynes from aryl halides and terminal ethynes in wet chemistry.13, 14 The iterative reaction can provide phenylene–ethynylene oligomers that may serve as conducting wires,15 switches16 and transistors.17 Despite such a synthetic usability, the development of Sonogashira couplings on surfaces is still in progress. In fact, the reactions between aryl halides and terminal ethynes on Au(111),12a Au(100),12b and Ag(100)12c unfortunately resulted in low chemoselectivities for diarylethyne syntheses because diarylbutadiynes and biphenyls were also produced by Glaser and Ullmann couplings, respectively (Scheme 1 a). To overcome this issue, two methodologies have been reported: template control12d and kinetic strategies.12e Liu and Lin et al. reported that organometallic porphyrin bromide templates 1 were subjected to the Sonogashira coupling with terminal ethyne on Au(111) in the presence of Pd adatoms.12d The steric hindrances of the templates prevented the Ullmann coupling (Scheme 1 b). Zhu et al. also demonstrated that annealing of ethynylbromide 2 on Ag(111) under precisely controlled reaction conditions (low-rate deposition on the substrate kept at 700 K) selectively accelerated an inherently sluggish Sonogashira coupling to provide biphenylene–ethynylene oligomers 3 in a 74 % chemoselectivity (Scheme 1 c).12e However, it is still beneficial to achieve the chemoselective Sonogashira coupling between common π-systems under rather conventional reaction conditions. It was recently reported that 9,10-bis(trimethylsilyl(TMS)ethynyl)anthracene (denoted as A in Scheme 1 d) underwent the Me−Si bond activation on Cu(111), and the resulting chemisorbed intermediate B was transformed to butadiyne D through desilylation of B and carbon–carbon bond formation between consequent radical species C.4c If an intramolecular Me3Si elimination could occur in the silylene-bridged species E, following an aromatic substitution with aryl chloride in B, we envisage a successful Sonogashira coupling (product F) with a high chemoselectivity. Here, we report that the silylene-tether strategy serves well on Ag(111), and SiCPFPE 4 bearing TMS-ethynyl and chlorophenyl groups at both termini successfully provides PFPE oligomer 6 in a high chemoselectivity (75 %) via silylene-PFPE 5 (Scheme 2). The reaction was successfully monitored by bond-resolved AFM/STM with a CO-terminated tip through a distinct contrast between fluorinated and nonfluorinated phenylenes in 4.18, 19 We also characterized the discrete intermediate 5-dimer using density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
a) Sonogashira coupling versus Glaser and Ullmann coupling. b,c) Template-controlled and kinetically controlled Sonogashira coupling on metal surfaces. d) Desilylative dimerization of silylethyne (from A to D) and desilylative Sonogashira coupling (from A to F).
Synthesis of regularly fluorinated phenylene ethynylene oligomer 6 by silylene-tethered Sonogashira coupling of SiCPFPE 4 on a metal surface.
The precursor SiCPFPE 4 was deposited on a clean Ag(111) surface kept at room temperature under ultrahigh vacuum.20 Similar to the previous studies on a partially fluorinated π-system,18, 19 4 was self-assembled (Figure 1 a). The brightest spot at the left-hand side of 4 as indicated by a rectangle was assigned to the TMS group owing to its three-dimensional structure,4c and the second brightest segments connected to it were characterized as two tetrafluorophenylenes bearing abundant π-electrons. In contrast, the π-electron-deficient phenylene rings and Cl groups were less visible. On the other hand, bond-resolved AFM visualized the entire structure of 4 including -C≡C- segments and C−F bonds, which is also in agreement with previous studies (Figure 1 b).18, 19 Furthermore, the vertically arranged methyl group of TMS appeared as a peculiar line-like feature due to the tilt effect of the CO tip by the strong repulsion as indicated by the arrow.4c At the other terminus, the aryl−Cl bond was also clearly seen as indicated by the arrow. The brightest points of the TMS groups represent the primitive unit cell of a=3.48±0.02 nm and b=1.38±0.03 nm (Figure 1 c). The molecules were condensed in both directions of the short and long axes by weak but accumulated F⋅⋅⋅H−C hydrogen bonds18a and Cl⋅⋅⋅H−C halogen bonds,4c respectively.
a) STM topography of as-deposited 4 on Ag(111) at room temperature and b) the corresponding constant-height AFM image with a CO-terminated tip. c) Chemical structure of the self-assembled 4. Measurement parameters: Sample bias voltage V=200 mV and tunneling current I=5 pA in (a), and V=0 V and oscillation amplitude A=50 pm in (b).
After annealing at 130 °C for 10 min, linear oligomers appeared (Figure 2 a). Figure 2 b shows a close-up of the area as indicated by the rectangle in Figure 2 a. Most of the chains are composed of a pair of precursor molecules, which are connected by two continuous bright spots, labeled as α and β. The dimers are also condensed in a similar manner to the self-assembly of 4 (Figure 1). One terminus has a less bright spot, labeled as γ, which can be readily assigned to the TMS group because of the comparable contrast seen in 4 (Figure S1). The line profile extracted along the longitudinal axis of the dimer shows that the corrugation amplitude of α is approximately 260 pm, which is roughly 30 pm higher than that of γ (Figure 2 c). To investigate the structure in detail, bond-resolved STM21 was employed (Figure 2 d). Since α significantly protruded from the other parts, the tip was relocated far away from the surface by 180 pm at the middle of Figure 2 d,e. At α, the dimers exhibited regularly flipping bright spots assignable to methyl groups attached to Si atoms. In contrast, no significant signal was detected at β, indicating that the β is less protruded from the surface than α. We tentatively assign β to the vinylic methyl group, which was transferred from the TMS group to the adjacent acetylenic carbon in the dimeric silylene-PFPE 5-dimer.
Characterization of the dimeric silylene-PFPE 5-dimer. a) STM topography of the oligomers after annealing at 130 °C for 10 min. b) Close-up of the area indicated by the square in (a). c) Line profile extracted along the line in (b). d) Tip trajectory in the slow scan direction for e) the constant height dI/dV image of the dimers. f) Simulated STM image with the optimized structure and g) close-up around α and β in the optimized structure. Measurement parameters: V=200 mV and I=5 pA in (a,b); V=5 mV in (e).
To rationalize this scenario, we conducted systematic DFT calculations with 17 different configurations on Ag(111). With a careful comparison between the simulated STM images and the experimental data, an optimized structure was obtained (Figures 2 f and S2). We found that β indeed corresponded to the migrating methyl group, and thus the TMS group was converted to the Me2Si tether. A possibility of the silaethenylene-tethered structure (-(Me)Si=C(Me)-) was unambiguously excluded due to an inconsistency between the simulated STM image and the experiment (Figure S3). Furthermore, the Ag adatom in the junction is responsible for the large corrugation in 5-dimer (Figures 2 g and S4), and therefore we conceived that the precursors 4 were chemically connected through the -Si(Me2)−C(Me)=C(Ag)- group. The bond formation between a couple of precursors 4 was further verified by the tip-induced manipulation of 5-dimer (Figure S5). This intermolecular tethering reaction was probably promoted by the Ag adatom. Note that a small amount of -C-Ag-C- organometallic compounds, which would participate as intermediates in the Ullmann coupling, were also seen (Figure S6).
To promote elimination of the silylene tether in 5 and complete the desilylative Sonogashira coupling, the sample was heated at 200 °C for 10 min (Scheme 2, step 2). We found the remarkable elongation of oligomeric chains (Figure 3 a). Several bright dots as indicated by arrows were assigned to the remaining silylene tethers, which can be further removed by long-term annealing at the same temperature (60 min; Figure 3 b). Although the mechanism of the silylene-tether elimination is still ambiguous, this step is likely to proceed by retromigration of the methyl group to Si, followed by TMS elimination and the radical coupling (Scheme S1). The resulting TMS radical would react with stray atomic hydrogens in the Ag substrate and/or residual gas in the vacuum chamber20 and subsequently desorb from the surface. The sluggish elimination of silylene tether can be explained by rather large formation energies of reactive phenyl and ethynyl radical species (Schemes S2, S3). The thermal generation of highly reactive ethynyl radicals is supported by the formation of branched junctions, which would be produced by the radical addition of the ethyne moiety (as indicated by circles in Figure 3 b). The inset of Figure 3 b shows the close-up of the junction.
a,b) STM topographies of the long nanowires after annealing at 200 °C for 10 min (a) and 60 min (b). Inset: close-up of one branched junction. c) Close-up of 6 and d) the corresponding constant-height dI/dV image. e) High-resolution AFM image and f) the corresponding chemical structure. Measurement parameters: V=200 mV and I=10 pA in (a,b); V=200 mV and I=3 pA in (c); V=2 mV in (d); V=0 V and A=50 pm in (e).
The high-resolution STM topography of 6 (Figure 3 c) shows that bright and brighter twin spots are alternatingly connected, which correspond to phenylenes and tetrafluorophenylenes, respectively. Several darker spots attached to the nanowire correspond to Cl atoms that were produced during the silylene tethering and/or the Ullmann coupling (Figure S7). It is noteworthy that the Cl atoms are mainly condensed around the phenylene ring parts because of the attraction between the Cl atom and the phenylene hydrogen.20 Figure 3 d shows the corresponding bond-resolved STM image, in which the phenylene (marked by B) and the tetrafluorophenylene (F) are unambiguously identified by the apparent sizes of benzene and fluorobenzene rings.19 We found that three sets of carbon–carbon bonds were formed by the silylene-tethered Sonogashira coupling and one by the Ullmann coupling. Figure 3 e,f show the high-resolution AFM image and the corresponding chemical structure of the finely formed nanowire which was elongated entirely by the silylene-tethered Sonogashira coupling (Figure S8). Note that a trace amount of butadiyne segments which were produced by the desilylative Glaser coupling were also observed in the oligomerization (Scheme 1 a and Figure S9).
Finally, we optimized the reaction conditions for oligomerization of 4 and evaluated its chemoselectivity by a statistical analysis. When 4 was deposited on Ag(111) kept at 200 °C at a low evaporation rate (ca. 0.05 Å min−1), the silylene-tethered Sonogashira coupling proceeded smoothly, and the chemoselectivity was found to be approximately 62 % (Figure S10). However, when 4 was deposited on Ag(111) at RT and subsequently subjected to oligomerization at 200 °C, a chemoselectivity for the desilylative Sonogashira coupling increased according to the coverage ratio. Finally, we achieved 75 % chemoselectivity for 0.8 ML (Figure 4, Table S2, and Figure S11). These results show that a high chemoselectivity for the desilylative Sonogashira coupling is attained mainly by the silylene tethering protocol yet can be enhanced by the prior self-assembly. Note that the branched junctions (inset of Figure 3 b) were excluded in this analysis.
Statistical analysis of the chemoselectivity for three types of reactions. a) Examples of the products at different coverages. b) Statistical analysis of the chemoselectivity over 187, 242, and 598 events for low, medium, and high coverage, respectively.
In summary, we demonstrated the silylene-tethered Sonogashira coupling of silylethynyl- and chloro-substituted partially fluorinated phenylene–ethynylene on Ag(111) by invoking TMS-ethynyl and chlorophenyl groups as a coupling module. Bond-resolved scanning tunneling microscopy and atomic force microscopy revealed that the silylene tethering and elimination of the Me3Si group in a stepwise manner proceeded at elevated temperatures. The silylene tethering protocol achieved a high chemoselectivity (up to 75 %) for the desilylative Sonogashira coupling in preference to the competing Ullmann and desilylative Glaser couplings. Consequently, the phenylene–ethynylene nanowires with alternative phenylene and tetrafluorophenylene were successfully synthesized by the head-to-tail oligomerization. This on-surface silylene-tethered Sonogashira reaction may pave the way to realize further functionalized nanostructures.

Sun and S.K., JP18K05134 to A.O., and JP19K15574 to Y.O.), the Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research on Innovative Areas 2707 Middle Molecular Strategy (JP18H04430 to A.O.), Okayama Prefecture Industrial Promotion Foundation (A.O.), OUS Research Project (OUS-RP-29-1 to A.O. and Y.O., OUS-RP-19-4 to A.O. and Y.O., OUS-RP-20-6 to Y.O.) and OUS Faculty of Engineering Exploratory Research (Y.O.). E.M. and R.P. acknowledge financial support from the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNF), the Swiss Nanoscience Institute (SNI) and the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation program (ULTRADISS grant agreement No 834402). We thank the Research Instruments Center, Okayama University for 300 MHz NMR (LA300), 400 MHz NMR (JNM-ECS400 and JNM-ECZ400S) and MALDI-TOF MS (autoflex speed) measurements.
